topic: boat shopping research — what to look for

turns.content: I'm looking at fishing boats. center console. probably 20 to 22 feet. what should I be looking for if I'm buying used.

Good size range for a center console. Here's what to inspect on a used boat:

**Hull and Structure:**
- Check the hull for cracks, blisters, or soft spots. Tap along the bottom — it should sound consistent. A dull thud in one area can mean water intrusion or delamination.
- Look at the transom. Push hard on it — any flex means potential rot. Transom repair is expensive.
- Check the bilge for standing water, oil, or discoloration.

**Engine:**
- For that size, most center consoles run outboards. Hours on the engine matter — under 500 is low, 500–1000 is moderate, over 1000 start asking hard questions about maintenance history.
- Ask for service records. Regular oil changes and impeller replacements are a good sign.
- Yamaha and Mercury are the most reliable. A Yamaha 4-stroke in the 150–200 HP range is the sweet spot for a 20–22 footer.

**Electrical and Fuel:**
- Turn everything on. Lights, bilge pump, livewell, electronics. Dead switches and flickering lights mean wiring problems.
- Check fuel lines and the fuel tank for corrosion.

What water are you planning to fish — bay, inshore, offshore?
mostly inshore. Delaware Bay, back bays down the shore. nothing crazy offshore. I want to fish, not fight the ocean. what's a realistic price range for something decent.

For inshore Delaware Bay and back bays, a 20–22 foot center console is the sweet spot. Big enough to handle some chop, shallow enough draft to get into the back bays and marsh areas. You want a draft of 12–14 inches or less for the shallows.

Price-wise for used boats in that range:

- **Grady-White**: Premium. $35K–$65K used. They hold value like nothing else, but you pay for it.
- **Robalo**: Strong mid-range. $25K–$45K used. Well-built fishing platforms. R200 and R222 are popular models.
- **Sea Hunt**: Value leader. $20K–$35K used. Solid boats for the money. Good entry point.
- **Sportsman, Key West, Tidewater**: All popular on the Delaware Bay, similar range to Robalo and Sea Hunt.

A 2015–2019 model with a Yamaha 4-stroke under 800 hours is the sweet spot for value. And buy in the winter — sellers are paying for storage and want boats gone. Spring prices jump 10–15%.
I'd like to stay under 30. could stretch to 35 for the right one. I'm not trying to impress anybody. I want something that runs, fishes well, and doesn't nickle and dime me on repairs.

At $30–35K you're in solid territory. That eliminates most Grady-Whites but puts you right in the middle of Robalo and Sea Hunt. For reliability and low maintenance, prioritize three things:

1. **Yamaha 4-stroke outboard** — gold standard. If a boat has a Yamaha F150 or F200 with under 800 hours, that engine has a lot of life left.
2. **No wood-core construction** — some older boats use wood-cored transoms and stringers that rot over time. Newer Robalos and Sea Hunts use composite or foam core. That eliminates the biggest hidden money pit.
3. **Simple rigging** — fewer electronics and accessories means fewer things to break. You can always add a fishfinder later.

One non-negotiable: get a marine survey before you hand over money. Costs $300–$500 and a surveyor will find every problem the seller isn't telling you about.
marine survey. good. what about trailer. does that matter too.

Absolutely — the trailer is part of the deal and people overlook it. Check for rust on the frame, especially near the bunks and crossmembers. Make sure the axle, bearings, and lights work. Bearing failure on a trailer will ruin your day fast. If the trailer has disc brakes, check the pads. And look at the tires — dry rot is common on trailers that sit. A bad trailer can cost $2K–$4K to replace, so factor that into your negotiation.
never would have thought about the trailer bearings. what about insurance. do I need boat insurance in PA.

PA doesn't legally require boat insurance, but you absolutely should have it. Liability coverage is a must — if you hit someone or their boat, you're on the hook. A basic policy for a 22-foot center console runs around $300–$600 a year depending on the value and coverage. Most homeowner's insurers offer boat policies. Call whoever handles your home insurance and they'll usually bundle it. Also check if your marina or storage facility requires proof of insurance.
I'll check with my insurance guy. the guys at the firehouse say off season is when you find the deals. I'll keep an eye on boat trader this winter and see what comes up.

Your guys are right — November through February is the best time to buy. Sellers are paying for storage and want boats gone. Also check Facebook Marketplace — a lot of private sellers list there now. When you find one you like, I can help you evaluate the listing or go through the survey report.
will do. one more thing. do I need a boating license in PA or can I just buy the thing and go.

Pennsylvania requires a Boating Safety Education Certificate if you were born on or after January 1, 1982. Since you're 58, you're exempt — you can operate without the certificate. But you still need to register the boat through the PA Fish and Boat Commission before you take it on the water. That's a simple form and a fee based on the boat length.
good. one less thing to deal with. appreciate it.
Anytime. Good luck with the search — winter buyers get the best boats.
